scenario: Redmine work session - audit the Issue contract before a teardown rework

map_quality.rationale: The answer correctly names Redmine, gives the Rails layout with concrete path groups (app/models, lib/redmine, lib/plugins/acts_as_*), and pins Issue at app/models/issue.rb:20 — a usable entry point. However it omits two of the three shared contracts entirely (Watcher, CustomValue), so the agent still needs exploratory work to locate those records and the specific mixin files that wire them in.

specificity.rationale: Issue gets a file:line citation and the layout paths are concrete; Attachment is named but without a file path, and Watcher and CustomValue are absent altogether — the three paths the rubric asks for (app/models/attachment.rb, app/models/watcher.rb, app/models/custom_value.rb) are almost entirely missing.

justification.rationale: lib/plugins/acts_as_* is labelled 'behavior mixins' but the answer never explains why the architecture uses them — there is no mention of polymorphic reuse, the pattern of many models opting in, or why the shared behaviors are factored out rather than inlined.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer expresses no uncertainty at all; it lists pieces as though fully verified without flagging anything it did not open or confirm, leaving the agent to discover gaps on its own.

relationship.rationale: The answer enumerates associated models (Attachment, Journal, TimeEntry, etc.) as a flat list but states no connecting relationship for any of them — no has_many :attachments as: :container, no polymorphic association, no mixin-opt-in path — so an agent cannot tell how any piece binds to Issue without re-reading the files.

steps.step: Map the Issue contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: All three contracts are mapped with mixin file:line and teardown semantics (`:destroy` vs `:delete_all`); the 'what to preserve' section names reciprocal back-references (time_entry.rb:25, journal.rb:27) and safe_attributes. An agent could start working with only targeted lookups into the stored-record files.

specificity.rationale: Mixin paths are fully concrete (lib/plugins/acts_as_attachable/lib/acts_as_attachable.rb:52, etc.), but the stored record files the rubric asks for — app/models/attachment.rb, app/models/watcher.rb, app/models/custom_value.rb — are never cited with file:line.

justification.rationale: The `:destroy`-vs-`:delete_all` distinction is explained with consequence (runs callbacks / skips them), but the answer never explains WHY the architecture factors shared behavior into reusable mixins with a polymorphic collaborator record rather than direct associations, which is the structural 'why' the rubric targets.

uncertainty.rationale: No hedging anywhere despite providing very specific line numbers (e.g. acts_as_customizable.rb:28, issue.rb:565); the agent is given no signal about which references were read vs inferred, leaving it to verify all of them blind.

relationship.rationale: Attachments get the full relation path ('as: :container, dependent: :destroy'), but watchers and custom_values are only described with their `dependent:` option — the polymorphic keys (`:watchable`, `:customized`) that bind the collaborator record to the host are not stated for those two contracts.

steps.step: Trace how an issue and its attached data are handled on change

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps trigger points, inline vs after-commit vs async layers with file:line for the callback chain, and correctly separates Journal-side notifications; but 'Dependent teardown fires here: journals/time_entries/attachments destroyed' is asserted without tracing the mixin wiring (which has_many :dependent declaration on which concern fires the delete), so an agent cannot safely rework teardown without re-reading the model.

specificity.rationale: Callbacks are concrete (issue.rb:116, :125, :132–134; journal.rb:66, :374, :385), but the teardown path — the actual has_many :dependent declarations in the shared mixins — is never cited by file:line; 'deleted' and 'destroyed' are asserted, not sourced.

justification.rationale: The answer lists the callback chain but does not explain WHY teardown is handled through shared mixin dependent associations (so every participant inherits the same cleanup); 'Dependent teardown fires here' is stated as a fact, not reasoned about.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere; the dependent: teardown semantics are asserted confidently despite the answer never opening the mixin files, leaving the agent no prompt to verify before acting.

relationship.rationale: The callback chain from trigger through after_commit to async Mailer is connected and readable; but the critical mixin-to-teardown path — Issue includes Attachable → has_many :attachments, as: :container, dependent: :destroy → AR cascades on destroy — is entirely absent, so the chain breaks exactly where it matters most for a teardown rework.

steps.step: Audit every dependent of the Issue contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps Issue's direct dependents across architectural layers (controllers, models, helpers, mailer) with good breadth, but never enumerates the three shared contracts (attachable/watchable/customizable) and which models participate in each — the rubric's explicit heart. Models like message.rb, news.rb, wiki_page.rb, board.rb, or document.rb appear nowhere, so the agent cannot audit which participants would break when the shared mixin changes.

specificity.rationale: What the answer does cover is named with concrete file:line (e.g. 'issues_controller.rb:45', 'issue_query.rb:375'), but the rubric's key specifics — participants in each contract named by file (app/models/message.rb, app/models/board.rb, app/models/time_entry.rb as watchable/attachable participants) — are absent or untethered from their contract.

justification.rationale: The grouping is by architectural layer (controller, model, helper), not by contract membership; there is no explanation that a model participates because it opts into a shared mixin, so the agent cannot infer which other models would be co-affected by a mixin change.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer never flags that a text search for the Issue class misses participants that reach the collaborator record only through the shared mixin's polymorphic association — the exact blindspot the rubric asks the answer to acknowledge so the agent knows to verify.

relationship.rationale: No entry states the mixin or polymorphic association (attachable / watchable / customizable, has_many … as: :container) that connects a participating model to the stored collaborator record; the answer lists direct Issue callers by architectural role only, leaving the connecting paths entirely implicit.

steps.step: Trace how issue identity and visibility are guarded

map_quality.rationale: Dense file:line coverage of every guard surface — request entry (application_controller.rb:394), core predicates (issue.rb:137/172/84), query layer (issue_query.rb:375/406), background mail (mailer.rb:603/638), notifications (issue.rb:1148/1160), autocomplete/context-menu (auto_completes_controller.rb:23, context_menus_controller.rb:27), and parent-journal re-guard (issue.rb:2071/2081). An agent could pick entry points and start without fresh exploration. Small gap: the answer never addresses whether Attachment/Watcher/CustomValue rows carry their own guards or purely inherit through the Issue visible scope, which is the rework audience's actual concern.

specificity.rationale: Guards are named with exact method signatures, file paths, and line numbers throughout; the SQL dimension of `visible_condition` (role `issues_visibility`, `is_private`, author/assignee, per-tracker `view_issues`) is itemized rather than glossed.

justification.rationale: The answer names what each guard checks but gives only partial WHY: `visible?` delegates to `allowed_to?(:view_issues, project)` implies host-record inheritance, but the answer never explicitly states the design principle that an attached row has no independent visibility, it inherits the host's. The agent gets the mechanism but not the invariant.

uncertainty.rationale: Only one implicit hedge — 'Gantt/Calendar render only what the underlying IssueQuery returns' — with no explicit flag that the agent should verify dependent: teardown semantics or whether any contract layer bypasses the visible scope. Guards that are implicitly delegated are presented as confirmed.

relationship.rationale: The answer traces request-entry → visible? → `allowed_to?(:view_issues, project)` and notification-fan-out → visible?(user) as connected paths, which is good. But the rubric axis is the chain from an attached-data row (attachment/watcher/custom_value) up to the host Issue's permission check, and that path is never assembled; the answer lists guard sites on Issue itself without connecting them back to the three collaborator records the rework touches.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a well-ranked, file:line map of Issue internals (IssuesController#destroy, callback chain, NestedSet, TimeEntry, IssueRelation) and includes a 'must re-verify' checklist, but stays almost entirely inside Issue.rb — it never fans out to enumerate which OTHER models are at risk across all three contracts (e.g., every model that includes acts_as_attachable or acts_as_watchable would be in blast radius if the mixin itself changes), so the agent still needs exploratory work to cover the full participant set.

specificity.rationale: File:line citations are dense and precise throughout: issues_controller.rb:431, issue.rb:124-134, issue_nested_set.rb:171/223, acts_as_attachable.rb:60, acts_as_customizable.rb:32, journal.rb:374, version.rb:255, changeset.rb:238, group.rb:116, user.rb:990 — all concrete. The 'lower risk' group lacks file:line and is a prose list, which is a minor gap.

justification.rationale: Most high-risk entries carry a tight causal note: IssuesController 'tightly coupled to the dependent: config', NestedSet 'corruption here is silent and hard to detect', acts_as_customizable 'attachment teardown interplay'. Medium-risk justifications are thinner ('time rollups', 'relation teardown affects scheduling') and the watcher contract gets no dedicated justification.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer projects consistent confidence with no explicit flags for participants reached only through the mixin or for teardown semantics it did not open; the lower-risk group is dismissed as 'read/render only' without acknowledging that mixin-only participants could be missed entirely by this Issue-centric search.

relationship.rationale: Some relationship terms appear inline — 'dependent: :destroy', 'belongs_to :issue', 'HABTM', 'delete_all' — but the answer never assembles the chain as 'mixin-change → shared polymorphic association → participant model'; the watcher contract fan-out is almost absent, and most entries name a coupling without tracing the path from the storage/teardown change through the shared mixin to the at-risk participant.

map_quality.rationale: The map covers all three shared-mixin files with line numbers, a broad grouping of participating models and controllers, a concrete test list (unit/functional/helpers/mailer), and specific fixture files — a teammate could plausibly land the change. The one gap: participating models are grouped by architectural area (controllers, models, reporting, rendering) rather than by which of the three contracts they participate in, so the agent still needs to mentally re-sort before touching each contract.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every entry carries file:line — acts_as_attachable.rb:60, acts_as_customizable.rb:28-36, issue.rb:41-46/124-134/260/2049, issues_controller_test.rb:431 area, test_bulk_update* :7729 — well above the threshold of vague 'edit the models' answers; the fixture list and test filenames are also concrete.

justification.rationale: The JSON's step2_contract block embeds the WHY for the three contracts (e.g., 'acts_as_attachable issue.rb:48 -> acts_as_attachable.rb:52 (attachments dependent:destroy)'), but the step7_change_map section itself does not re-state why each file under review/touch belongs to this edit — Journal, TimeEntry, IssueRelation, etc. are listed without an explicit link to which storage or teardown contract they belong to or what the change does to them.

uncertainty.rationale: Step 7 carries no explicit uncertainty flags; the closest is step6's 'must_reverify' list and the 'todo modes' comment for the destroy test, but these appear in prior steps rather than as forward-looking caveats in the change map itself — the agent would not know which fixtures or mixin semantics to double-check before committing.

relationship.rationale: The JSON provides some explicit chains — 'acts_as_attachable issue.rb:48 -> acts_as_attachable.rb:52 (attachments dependent:destroy)' and 'Journal dependent:destroy + send_notification journal.rb:20/374' — but the tests and fixtures section lists files without stating which shared-mixin contract each test exercises or how the fixture connects to the teardown change, leaving the chain from storage-change through mixin to test implicit.
